content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF
ROBERT R. WELLINGTON
857 CASS STREET, SUITE D
MONTEREY, CALIFORNIA 93940
(408) 373-8733

ROBERT R. WELLINGTON
KENNETH D. BUCHERT

FACSIMILE
(408) 373-7106

October 19, 1990

John Wallace, Esq.
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: City of Marina; Your File No. I-90-249

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     Enclosed are supplementary materials which you requested concerning the question of whether the City of Marina has "jurisdiction" over the portion of Fort Ord within its boundaries.

     First is a letter from the Staff Judge Advocate of Fort Ord in which he explains why the Federal Government has exclusive legislative jurisdiction over the Military Reservation (Exhibit A). His conclusion is based on the case law cited, but it is also supported by 10 United States Code Section 2683 (Exhibit B), which is the statutory basis for the relinquishment of legislative jurisdiction over Federal lands to a state. This office has been unable to locate any record of such relinquishment of legislative jurisdiction over Fort Ord and it must therefore be presumed that the mere act of annexation did not change the powers of the various governmental agencies but simply transferred those powers held by the State to the City (Howard v. Commissioners (1953) 344 U.S. 624).

     AR (Army Regulation) 405-20 provides a definition and discussion of the term "Legislative Jurisdiction" as well as "Exclusive Legislative Jurisdiction" (Exhibit C). A review of these terms shows clearly that the Federal Government has all of the authority of the State which has not been reserved to the State, to enact, execute and enforce general legislation in that area. In 23 Op. A.G. Cal. 14 (1954), the Attorney General reviewed the jurisdiction issue as it specifically related to Fort Ord and concluded that the United States had exclusive jurisdiction over the area. Further review of the statutes cited in that opinion, Exhibit D, indicates that the State has reserved unto itself the power to tax and to serve civil and criminal process, thus implying that these are the only powers which are so reserved.

AR 405-25 (Exhibit E) further reinforces the continued and
substantial extent of Federal jurisdiction after annexation by
stating "Annexation does not alter the existing Federal
jurisdiction over Army controlled land and does not result in any
interference with official Army activities or functions because
these are protected by Federal constitutional immunity."

Hopefully these materials will assist you in your review of
this extremely important matter and will allow you to conclude that
the position of the City of Marina in this regard is correct. If
you have any questions or need any further information, please feel
free to contact this office.

Thank you for your continued courtesy and assistance in this
matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Kenneth D. Buchert
Assistant City Attorney
City of Marina

KDB:pb
Enclosures
cc: City Council
    City Manager
    Fort Ord Staff Judge Advocate

City of Marina

241 HILLCREST AVENUE
MARINA, CA 93933
TELEPHONE (408) 384-3715
FAX (408) 384-0425

August 7, 1990


Kathryn E. Donovan, Esq.,
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95815

Re:  City of Marina Request for Reconsideration of Interpretation
     Your File No. I-90-242

Dear Ms. Donovan:

The City of Marina has reviewed the response of your office dated
May 7, 1990, to the City Attorney's letter of March 20, 1990
requesting advice. Because of the severe impact of the conclusion
that Regulation 18703.1 is not available for the City of Marina.
I have been authorized and directed by the City Council to present
additional facts to the Commission in order to show that the City
does not have "jurisdiction" over either the area or population of
the City, which is contained within Port Ord and to take the
necessary actions to make Regulation 18703.1 available to the City.

It would appear that the Commission's conclusion is based on the
definition of jurisdiction found on page 2 of the advice letter and
the statement immediately following "under your facts, while the
city council's authority with respect to the area in question is
limited in comparison with the council's authority over the rest
of the city, the council, nevertheless, has some authority and
official duties with respect to the region". This statement is
contrary to the facts as was pointed out in the City Attorney's
letter of March 20, 1990, which stated "the City has absolutely no
control over the population and territory found on Fort Ord" and
"the area and the population residing within it are not subject to
any of the laws of the City of Marina". The fact that persons
residing in the area vote in City elections does not support a
contention that the City has authority over the population but is
rather a quirk of the law. Those voters residing on Fort Ord have
the right to vote for the City Council but the City Council has no
power over those voters.

Kathryn E. Donovan, Esq.    August 7, 1990    Page Two


The difficulty of not having the public generally exception apply
to the City of Marina was vividly illustrated by a recent meeting
of the Planning Commission.  On July 23, 1990, the Commission was
scheduled to hear a matter concerning a major renovation of a small
shopping center in the city.  The Commission consists of seven
members with a quorum being five.  At that meeting two members were
absent and four were disqualified due to living within 2,500 feet
of the property under consideration.   A quorum could not be
convened because under Regulation 18701 only two Commissioners
could be selected by lot meaning only a total of three
Commissioners would be present.  As a result the action was
required to be continued due to lack of a quorum.  The difficulty
then arose as to when the matter could be considered because one of
the absent members was on record as stating he would most likely be
absent from Commission meetings for two months.  Even assuming that
Commissioner attended the next meeting, there is no assurance that
another, non-disqualified member would not be absent.  In short, if
any one of the three non-disqualified members is absent, the matter
could not be considered.   This situation is not fair to the
developer nor fair to the Commissioners who must make their work
and recreation plan fit a schedule which is unpredictable and out
of their control in order to participate in a frequently thankless
job of sitting on the Planning Commission.

The above situation is not unusual in this City and has, in fact,
occurred on other occasions.  Both the City Council and the
Planning Commission have, because of the small area which
encompasses where elected and appointed officials reside,
encountered similar problems of disqualification due to the
proximity of their members' interests in real property to a
project.

The City is not asking that this exception be expanded.  Rather,
the City is asking that the Commission look again at what the term
"jurisdiction" means when the boundaries of the City do not
coincide with the control and authority of the City.  Such an
examination of the facts regarding the City of Marina's
"jurisdiction" over territory within Port Ord will reveal that the
City has absolutely no control over that territory.  I am sure that
any inquiry to the Commanding General of Fort Ord, the Department
of the Army or any appropriate office of the Federal Government
would show that it was never intended that a City would have any
"right to exercise official authority" over federal territory
annexed to a city as has occurred here.

Kathryn E. Donovan, Esq.     August 7, 1990     Page Three


The City requests that the Commission reconsider its interpretation
of the term "jurisdiction" as it applies to the applicability of
Regulation 18703.1 or in the alternative adopt new language making
the public generally exception applicable to cities which exceed
the population and/or area limits of Regulation 18703.1 solely due
to annexation of federal property.

The City would welcome the opportunity to discuss this matter
further with the Commission and/or the Chairman. To that end the
City requests that a meeting be arranged in order to allow a
delegation from the City to present further information and answer
questions at the earliest possible date.

I look forward to hearing from your office as soon as possible. If
you have any questions on this matter, please feel free to contact
either the City Manager, John Longley, at (408) 384-3715 or the
City Attorney, Robert Wellington, (408) 373-8733.

Very Truly yours,


George J. Takahashi, Mayor

GJT/mh

cc:  Senator Henry Mello
     Assemblyman Sam Farr
     City Council
     City Planning Commission
     City Attorney
     City Manager
     Planning Director
